Get Secure Exec running in a few minutes.
Secure Exec: a fully virtualized runtime for executing untrusted code with zero host escapes.
Guided tour of the secure-exec API surface.

import { Tabs, TabItem, CardGrid, LinkCard } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

A tour of the `NodeRuntime` class, the entry point for booting a VM and running
guest JavaScript end-to-end.

## Install

<Tabs>
  <TabItem label="npm">
  ```bash
  npm install secure-exec
  ```
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="bun">
  ```bash
  bun add secure-exec
  ```
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="pnpm">
  ```bash
  pnpm add secure-exec
  ```
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="yarn">
  ```bash
  yarn add secure-exec
  ```
  </TabItem>
</Tabs>

## Create a runtime

`NodeRuntime.create(options?)` boots a VM and returns a ready-to-use runtime.
All options are optional.

```ts
import { NodeRuntime } from "secure-exec";

const rt = await NodeRuntime.create({
  env: { GREETING: "hello from the VM env" },
  cwd: "/home/user",
});
```

*[See Full Example](https://github.com/rivet-dev/secure-exec/tree/main/examples/docs/sdk-overview)*

Every `create()` option:

- `env`: environment variables visible to guest processes.
- `cwd`: initial working directory for guests. Defaults to `/home/user`.
- `permissions`: capability policy, merged over a secure default that denies
  network access. See [Permissions](#permissions).
- `files`: seed files into the VM filesystem before boot, keyed by absolute
  guest path. Values are `string | Uint8Array`. Parent dirs are created as needed.
- `mounts`: project host directories into the VM, Docker-style and read lazily.
- `tools`: register host-side tools the guest can invoke as shell commands.
- `commandsDir`: override the directory of WASM command binaries (the guest
  `sh`). Defaults to the in-repo build or `SECURE_EXEC_WASM_COMMANDS_DIR`.
- `loopbackExemptPorts`: guest ports allowed to accept non-loopback connections.

```ts
const rt = await NodeRuntime.create({
  // Seed bytes into the in-memory VFS.
  files: { "/home/user/data.json": '{"ok":true}' },
  // Mount a host dir, lazily and read-only by default.
  mounts: [
    {
      guestPath: "/home/user/node_modules/typescript",
      hostPath: "/abs/path/node_modules/typescript",
      readOnly: true,
    },
  ],
});
```

## Execute code

Two methods run sandboxed code. Guest `code` runs as an ES module, so `import`
and top-level `await` both work.

`exec()` runs code and captures `stdout`, `stderr`, and `exitCode`:

```ts
const execResult = await rt.exec(`
  import os from "node:os";
  console.log("platform:", os.platform());
  console.error("greeting:", process.env.GREETING);
`);
// { stdout: string, stderr: string, exitCode: number }
```

`run<T>()` additionally decodes a JSON-serializable `value`. The guest calls
`globalThis.__return(value)`; if it never does, `value` is `undefined`:

```ts
const runResult = await rt.run<{ sum: number }>(`
  globalThis.__return({ sum: 2 + 40 });
`);
console.log(runResult.value?.sum); // 42
// { value?: T, stdout, stderr, exitCode }
```

## Per-run options

`exec()` and `run()` accept the same per-call options:

- `env`: extra env merged over the VM env.
- `cwd`: working directory for this run.
- `stdin`: `string | Uint8Array` piped to the guest's stdin.
- `timeout`: abort the run after N milliseconds.
- `signal`: an `AbortSignal`; on abort the guest process is killed (kernel
  delivers `SIGTERM`) and the call rejects with the abort reason.
- `onStdout` / `onStderr`: `(chunk: Uint8Array) => void`, called per chunk as
  output is produced. The full output is still returned in the result.

```ts
const result = await rt.run<string>(
  `
  let input = "";
  for await (const chunk of process.stdin) input += chunk;
  globalThis.__return(input.trim().toUpperCase());
  `,
  {
    stdin: "piped through stdin",
    timeout: 10_000,
    onStdout: (chunk) => process.stdout.write(new TextDecoder().decode(chunk)),
  },
);
```

```ts
// Cancel an in-flight run from the outside.
const controller = new AbortController();
const pending = rt.exec("while (true) {}", { signal: controller.signal });
controller.abort();
await pending.catch((err) => err.name); // "AbortError"
```

## Spawn a long-running process

`spawn()` starts a guest program and returns a live `NodeRuntimeProcess` handle
immediately, without waiting for it to exit. Use it for dev servers and other
guests that do not run to completion. It takes the same per-run options.

```ts
const server = await rt.spawn(`
  import http from "node:http";
  http.createServer((_, res) => res.end("ok")).listen(3000);
`);

server.pid;                       // guest process id
server.exitCode;                  // number once exited, else null
server.writeStdin("data");        // feed stdin
server.closeStdin();              // signal end-of-input
server.kill("SIGTERM");           // signal name or number; defaults to SIGTERM
await server.wait();              // resolves with the exit code
```

## Drive HTTP into the VM (host to guest)

`fetch(port, input)` sends an HTTP request to a guest server listening inside
the VM and reads the response back on the host. The request never leaves the VM,
so it works even when guest network egress is denied.

```ts
const res = await rt.fetch(3000, {
  method: "POST",                 // defaults to "GET"
  path: "/api/users?limit=10",
  headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
  body: '{"name":"ada"}',         // string | Uint8Array
});
// { status, statusText, headers, body }; headers lower-cased, body UTF-8 text
console.log(res.status, res.body);
```

## Host tools

Register host-side tools the guest invokes as named shell commands. The guest
invocation round-trips back to the host `handler`, whose return value is
delivered to the guest. Provide tools at `create()`, or add them to a live
runtime with `registerTools()`.

```ts
await rt.registerTools({
  add: {
    description: "Add two numbers",
    inputSchema: {
      type: "object",
      properties: { a: { type: "number" }, b: { type: "number" } },
      required: ["a", "b"],
    },
    handler: ({ a, b }: { a: number; b: number }) => ({ sum: a + b }),
  },
});

await rt.exec(`
  import { execFileSync } from "node:child_process";
  const out = execFileSync("add", ["add", "--json", JSON.stringify({ a: 2, b: 3 })]);
  console.log(out.toString());
`);
```

- Tools passed to `create({ tools })` auto-grant the `tool` permission scope when
  you set no `tool` policy of your own.
- When registering on a live runtime, ensure the `tool` scope is granted (e.g.
  `permissions: { tool: "allow" }` on `create()`).

## Read and write VM files

Move bytes in and out of the VM's virtual filesystem after boot. The host
filesystem is never touched.

```ts
await rt.writeFile("/home/user/out.txt", "hello");  // string | Uint8Array
const bytes = await rt.readFile("/home/user/out.txt"); // Uint8Array
console.log(new TextDecoder().decode(bytes));
```

## Permissions

The `permissions` option controls what guest code can access. It defaults to a
secure policy that denies network access while leaving the `fs`, `childProcess`,
`process`, and `env` scopes enabled. Your policy is merged over that default, so
a partial policy works: `{ network: "allow" }` grants the network while keeping
the execution essentials in place. Scopes are `fs`, `network`, `childProcess`,
`process`, `env`, and `tool`, each set to `"allow"`, `"deny"`, or a rule set.

Because Secure Exec runs each program as a guest `node` process, `childProcess`
must stay allowed for `exec()` / `run()` to work. The snippet below restates the
secure baseline explicitly:

```ts
const rt = await NodeRuntime.create({
  permissions: {
    fs: "allow",
    childProcess: "allow",
    network: "deny",
  },
});
```

See [Permissions](/docs/features/permissions) for the full policy shape.

## Clean up

Always release the VM and underlying sidecar when done:

```ts
await rt.dispose();
```
Spawn child processes from sandboxed code.
How sandboxed code resolves and loads modules.
Bound and cancel guest execution with exec/run timeouts and AbortSignal, plus the memory, payload, CPU-time, and timing limits the VM enforces.
Control what sandboxed code can access on the host.
Capture console output from sandboxed code.
The host environment guest code sees (the full Node.js surface today), plus the platform ladder (node, browser, neutral, bare) the kernel models.

Guest JavaScript runs with the **full Node.js emulation surface**: the `process`
and `Buffer` globals, the `node:*` builtin modules, npm module resolution, and a
virtualized Node process identity. This is what `NodeRuntime` boots, and today it
is the only platform Secure Exec exposes.

Guest code runs inside a kernel-backed V8 isolate with zero host escapes. Even
with the full Node surface available, every syscall (filesystem, network, child
process) goes through the kernel, never the real host.

```ts
import { NodeRuntime } from "secure-exec";

const rt = await NodeRuntime.create();

try {
	const probe = await rt.run<{
		platform: string;
		hasProcess: boolean;
		hasBuffer: boolean;
		nodeVersion: string;
		sha256: string;
		joinedPath: string;
	}>(`
		const { createHash } = await import("node:crypto");
		const { join } = await import("node:path");

		const sha256 = createHash("sha256").update("secure-exec").digest("hex");

		globalThis.__return({
			platform: typeof process !== "undefined" ? process.platform : "(no process)",
			hasProcess: typeof process !== "undefined",
			hasBuffer: typeof Buffer !== "undefined",
			nodeVersion: typeof process !== "undefined" ? process.versions.node : "(none)",
			sha256,
			joinedPath: join("/home/user", "report.txt"),
		});
	`);

	console.log(probe.value);
} finally {
	await rt.dispose();
}
```

*[See Full Example](https://github.com/rivet-dev/secure-exec/tree/main/examples/docs/feat-runtime-platform)*

Output, the guest host environment as seen from inside the isolate:

```text
{
  platform: 'linux',
  hasProcess: true,
  hasBuffer: true,
  nodeVersion: '22.0.0',
  sha256: 'fa7ce60dac0cc1bfe7424a68e47ad3d712345cf936431bb147cd5f5de0371a4a',
  joinedPath: '/home/user/report.txt'
}
```

:::note[Guest `run()` code is wrapped in an async function]
Code passed to `rt.run()` executes inside an `async` wrapper, so it can use
top-level `await` but is not a module body: use dynamic `await import("node:â€¦")`,
not a top-level `import`. Code passed to `rt.exec()` runs as a real ES module,
where static `import` is fine.
:::

## Host environment (the `node` surface)

Available to guest code on the default platform:

- **Node globals**: `process`, `Buffer`, `require`, `module`, `__dirname`, `__filename`.
- **`node:*` builtins**: `fs`, `path`, `crypto`, `http`, `net`, `os`, `child_process`, `dns`, and the rest of the Node standard library.
- **Node identity**: virtualized `process.versions` (Node `22.0.0`), `process.platform` (`linux`), `execPath`, and `pid`/`ppid`/`uid`/`gid`.
- **Web platform**: `fetch`, `URL`, `TextEncoder`/`TextDecoder`, WebCrypto, `structuredClone`, `Blob`, `AbortController`.
- **Universal primitives**: `console`, timers (`setTimeout`/`setInterval`/â€¦), `queueMicrotask`.
- **Language + Wasm**: the ECMAScript spec globals plus `WebAssembly`.

Every one of these is kernel-backed. `fetch` and `node:net`/`node:http` route
through the kernel socket table (and are denied by default until you grant
`network`); `node:fs` sees only the VM's virtual filesystem; `node:child_process`
spawns kernel-managed processes.

### Seeding the host environment

`NodeRuntime.create()` shapes what the guest sees before and after boot:

```ts
const rt = await NodeRuntime.create({
	env: { API_BASE: "https://example.test" },   // guest process env
	cwd: "/home/user",                            // default working dir
	files: { "/root/data.json": '{"ok":true}' },  // seed VFS bytes
	mounts: [                                      // project host dirs, Docker-style, lazy
		{ guestPath: "/root/node_modules/typescript", hostPath: "/abs/typescript", readOnly: true },
	],
	permissions: { network: "allow" },            // merged over secure default (network denied)
	tools: {                                       // host capabilities the guest calls as commands
		add: {
			description: "Add two numbers",
			inputSchema: { type: "object", properties: { a: { type: "number" }, b: { type: "number" } }, required: ["a", "b"] },
			handler: ({ a, b }: { a: number; b: number }) => ({ sum: a + b }),
		},
	},
	loopbackExemptPorts: [3000],                   // let non-loopback connections reach this port
	commandsDir: "/abs/wasm/commands",             // override the WASM `sh`/coreutils dir
});
```

- `files` copies bytes into the VFS up front; `mounts` reads host trees lazily through the VFS, so large `node_modules` are not copied as a blob.
- `permissions` merges over a secure default (`fs`/`childProcess`/`process`/`env` allowed, `network` denied), so `{ network: "allow" }` is enough to opt in.
- `tools` auto-grants the `tool` scope when you set no `tool` policy; the guest invokes a tool by name with `--json` input over `node:child_process`.

After boot, the same surface is reachable on the live runtime:

```ts
await rt.writeFile("/root/late.json", '{"added":"after boot"}');
const bytes = await rt.readFile("/root/late.json"); // Uint8Array
await rt.registerTools({ now: { description: "epoch ms", handler: () => Date.now() } });
```

### Running and driving guest programs

- `rt.exec(code, options)` runs an ESM program to completion, returns `{ stdout, stderr, exitCode }`.
- `rt.run<T>(code, options)` runs a program that calls `globalThis.__return(value)` and decodes it as `result.value` (plus `stdout`/`stderr`/`exitCode`).
- `rt.spawn(code, options)` starts a long-running guest and returns a live `NodeRuntimeProcess` (`pid`, `writeStdin`, `closeStdin`, `kill`, `wait`, `exitCode`).
- `rt.fetch(port, input)` drives an HTTP request from the host into a guest listener and returns `{ status, statusText, headers, body }`, even with egress denied.
- `rt.waitForListener(query, options)` / `rt.findListener(query)` resolve guest TCP listeners by `port`/`host`/`path`.

`exec`, `run`, and `spawn` accept the same per-run options:

```ts
const controller = new AbortController();
const result = await rt.exec(source, {
	env: { EXTRA: "1" },                  // merged over VM env
	cwd: "/tmp",
	stdin: "piped to the program",        // string | Uint8Array
	timeout: 5000,                        // kill after N ms
	signal: controller.signal,            // abort -> SIGTERM, call rejects AbortError
	onStdout: (chunk) => process.stdout.write(chunk), // Uint8Array chunks, live
	onStderr: (chunk) => process.stderr.write(chunk),
});
```

Host-to-guest dev-server pattern:

```ts
const server = await rt.spawn(`
	import http from "node:http";
	http.createServer((_, res) => res.end("ok")).listen(3000);
`);
const listener = await rt.waitForListener({ port: 3000 });
const res = await rt.fetch(listener.port ?? 3000, { path: "/" });
server.kill();
await server.wait();
```

## The platform ladder

The kernel models the guest host environment as a ladder, using esbuild's
vocabulary (`node` / `browser` / `neutral`) plus `bare` for the language-only
tier esbuild has no name for. `NodeRuntime` always runs the top rung (`node`);
the lower rungs are described here for context and are **not currently selectable
in Secure Exec** (see the note below).

| Capability | `node` | `browser` | `neutral` | `bare` |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Node globals | âś… | No | No | No |
| `node:*` builtins | âś… | No | No | No |
| Node identity | âś… | No | No | No |
| Web platform | âś… | âś… | No | No |
| Universal primitives | âś… | âś… | âś… | No |
| Language + Wasm | âś… | âś… | âś… | âś… |

- **`node`** (the default): full Node.js compatibility. Nothing removed.
- **`browser`**: a browser/Deno-like runtime. The Node surface is gone; web-standard globals remain. `crypto` is the WebCrypto object (`crypto.subtle`, `crypto.getRandomValues`), *not* the `node:crypto` module, so `crypto.randomBytes`/`crypto.createHash` are absent.
- **`neutral`**: universal primitives only (`console`, timers, `queueMicrotask`) plus the language. No `fetch`/`URL`/WebCrypto, no Node.
- **`bare`**: language only: the ECMAScript spec globals plus `WebAssembly`. No `console`, no timers, no `fetch`. The caller provides any host functionality the guest needs.

`WebAssembly` stays available on **every tier**, including `bare`. Compilation
happens inside the isolate and is not a host escape.

:::caution[Platform selection is not configurable in Secure Exec yet]
The kernel's wire protocol carries a `jsRuntime` config with `platform`,
`moduleResolution`, and `allowedBuiltins` fields. The tiers are
real in the kernel, but `NodeRuntime.create()` does **not** plumb those fields
through: it always boots the VM on the `node` platform with full Node module
resolution. There is no `NodeRuntime.create()` option to select
`browser`/`neutral`/`bare`, change module resolution, or restrict the builtin
allow-list today. The sections below document the intended model for those fields,
not a `create()` option you can pass.
:::

## `moduleResolution`: how imports resolve

An independent axis of the wire config (any combination with `platform` is
valid). Like esbuild, the default is full Node resolution even for non-`node`
platforms, which is exactly what `NodeRuntime` uses.

| `moduleResolution` | `import "pkg"` | `import "./x.js"` | `node:*` |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `node` (default) | âś… | âś… | âś… |
| `relative` | No | âś… | No |
| `none` | No | No | No |

(`import "pkg"` = bare/`node_modules` specifier; `import "./x.js"` = relative or
absolute path; `node:*` follows the platform / `allowedBuiltins`.)

- **`node`**: standard Node resolution: the `node_modules` ancestor walk, `exports`/`imports`/conditions, and `realpath`/symlink following.
- **`relative`**: only relative and absolute paths resolve from the VFS; bare package specifiers and `node:*` builtins do not.
- **`none`**: nothing resolves. Any `import` or `require` (including relative) fails. Produces a single self-contained entrypoint module, useful for locked-down evaluation of one script.

## `allowedBuiltins`: restrict Node builtins (node platform only)

When `platform` is `node`, narrow which `node:*` builtin modules guest code may
import:

- **omitted**: the engine default allow-list (what `NodeRuntime` uses).
- **`[]`**: deny **all** builtins.
- **`["path", "fs"]`**: allow exactly these (a root name like `fs` also covers `node:fs/promises`).

`allowedBuiltins` is only meaningful under `platform: "node"`; the other
platforms deny all `node:*` builtins regardless. Unknown builtin names are
rejected.

Give AI agents a secure code-execution tool that runs untrusted code in a sandbox and returns structured results.
Sandboxed code reads and writes a fully virtual filesystem that lives inside the kernel, isolated from the host disk.
Give AI agents a single code-execution tool instead of individual MCP tools. The LLM writes code that chains tool calls, executed safely in Secure Exec.
Run user-authored plugins in isolation with explicit permissions.
Run user-provided server-style code (HTTP servers, request handlers) inside a secure isolate.
